title: First Steps on the Mat
pillar_name: Physical Literacy, Martial Arts & Human Performance

required.title: [Equipment] Grappling mats or thick gymnastics mats
[Equipment] Boxing gloves (youth size, 6–8 oz) and focus mitts
[Instruction] A qualified BJJ, wrestling, or martial arts instructor

suggested.title: [Video] YouTube: Gracie Bullyproof or similar kids' BJJ programs

supplementary.title: [Video] YouTube: Wrestling basics for kids (age-appropriate instructionals)

competence_markers.description: The student follows mat etiquette consistently — bowing on, shaking hands, listening, maintaining hygiene — without reminders
The student is comfortable with physical contact — being held, pinned, grabbed — without panic or aggression
The student can demonstrate a basic wrestling stance and level change
The student can identify guard, mount, and side control, and explain which positions are advantageous
The student understands and respects the tap — both tapping when caught and releasing immediately when a partner taps
The student can throw a jab and cross on pads with reasonable form — straight punches, hands returning to guard position
The student can articulate that de-escalation and avoidance come before physical defense
The student's behavior on the mat — toward partners, under frustration, when losing — reflects developing self-control and respect

cross_connections.description: Mat etiquette is IC1 character formation in physical practice. Following rules, respecting others, and controlling impulses on the mat directly trains the habits from IC1.1.
Getting pinned, submitted, or outworked on the mat is controlled adversity. Learning to respond to loss with curiosity rather than anger is IC3 anti-fragility in action.
Falling safely, rolling, and balance skills transfer directly to martial arts. The forward roll from 2.1.1-A1 is the same forward roll used when taken down in wrestling.
De-escalation awareness connects directly to the awareness habits in 2.1.5. Knowing when to avoid, when to leave, and when to act is both a martial skill and a tactical one.
Helping newer students on the mat — rolling lighter, showing them basics, encouraging them — is the earliest form of leadership practice.

content.why_this_matters:
Have you ever watched two puppies wrestle? They roll around, grab each other, pin each other down, get back up, and do it again. They are not trying to hurt each other. They are learning how their bodies work when they are tangled up with someone else. They are learning what it feels like to push and be pushed, to hold and be held, to lose a position and scramble to get it back.

That is what you are going to learn in this packet — but better, because you are a human being, and you get to learn from teachers who have spent years understanding how the body works when it meets another body. You are going to learn to wrestle, to grapple, to throw a proper punch at a pad, and to move with another person in a way that is controlled and purposeful.

This is not about fighting. Fighting is what happens when people are angry and untrained. What you are learning is much more valuable than fighting. You are learning how to stay calm when someone grabs you. You are learning how to control someone without hurting them. You are learning that the best response to a bully is not to swing wildly — it is to know, deep in your bones, that you can handle yourself, so you do not panic when someone tries to scare you.

The most important thing in this packet is not a punch or a takedown. It is what happens inside your head. When you train on the mat, you will sometimes lose. Someone will pin you. Someone will catch you in a hold. That is not failing. That is learning. Every time someone catches you with something, you learn what not to do next time. The children who learn the most are the ones who get caught the most — because they are rolling with people who are better than them, and they are paying attention.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Comfort with physical contact** — being grabbed, held, pinned, and pushed without panic or distress
2. **Basic wrestling positions** — stance, level changes, the concept that position matters more than strength
3. **Basic BJJ (Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu) positions** — guard, mount, side control, and the concept of tapping
4. **Basic boxing mechanics** — stance, jab, cross, keeping hands up, hitting pads (not people)
5. **Mat etiquette** — bowing on, shaking hands, respecting training partners, following the rules of the training space
6. **De-escalation as the first martial skill** — walking away is not weakness; it is the smartest move you can make
7. **The habit of regular physical training with a partner** — learning that practice with another person is how skills are built
content.core_content:
### Part 1: The Mat Is a Special Place

When you step onto the training mat, you are entering a place with rules. These rules exist for one reason: so that everyone can train hard, learn a lot, and go home safe.

**The rules of the mat:**

- **Bow or nod when you step on.** This is not religious. It is a sign of respect — for the training space, for your partners, and for the art you are learning. Every martial art in the world has some version of this. It means: I am here to train, and I take this seriously.
- **Shake hands with your partner before and after every round.** Look them in the eye. This means: we are partners, not enemies. We are here to help each other learn.
- **Keep your nails trimmed.** Long fingernails scratch. Long toenails scratch. Your training partner should not go home with scratches because you did not prepare for class.
- **Be clean.** Shower before training. Wear a clean shirt or gi. The mats are a shared space, and hygiene is a basic act of respect for the people who share it with you.
- **Listen when the instructor is talking.** Not partway. All the way. Martial arts instruction is often about safety — if you miss the instruction, you or your partner might get hurt.

These are IC1 lessons in a physical setting. How you behave on the mat reveals your character. A student who follows these rules without being reminded is a student who is building real discipline.

### Part 2: Wrestling — Your Body Against Another Body

Wrestling is the oldest martial art in the world. Before there were swords, before there were boxing gloves, before there were fancy kicks, humans wrestled. They grabbed each other, tried to take each other down, and tried to hold each other on the ground. Every civilization in history has had some form of wrestling.

You are going to start at the very beginning.

**Stance:** Stand with your feet about shoulder-width apart, one foot slightly in front of the other. Bend your knees a little — you should feel like you are sitting on an invisible chair, but just barely. Keep your hands up, about chest level, elbows in. This is your fighting stance. It keeps you balanced and ready to move.

Why does stance matter? Because a person standing straight up with their feet together can be pushed over by a toddler. A person in a good stance is stable, mobile, and hard to move. Try it: stand straight up with your feet together and have someone push your chest. Now get into your wrestling stance and have them push again. Feel the difference? Stance is the foundation.

**Level changes:** From your stance, bend your knees deeper — dropping your hips toward the ground while keeping your back straight. This is a level change. It is the setup for every takedown in wrestling. When you change levels, you get under your opponent's center of gravity, which is how a smaller person can take down a bigger person. Practice this: stance, level change, back to stance. Do it until it is smooth and fast.

**Positional awareness:** In wrestling, position is everything. If you are on top of someone, you are winning. If you are on bottom, you need to get back to top or back to your feet. At this age, you are going to learn the basic concept: top is better than bottom, and staying on your feet is better than being taken down. When you wrestle with a partner, pay attention to where your body is relative to theirs. Are you on top? Underneath? To the side? Getting used to knowing where you are is the first step to controlling where you go.

**Rough-and-tumble games:** Before formal wrestling drills, you will play wrestling games. King of the hill — try to push your partner off a mat while they try to push you off. Sumo — start in a circle, first one to step out or touch the ground with anything but their feet loses. These games teach the core of wrestling — balance, leverage, and effort — without requiring any technique at all.

### Part 3: BJJ — The Gentle Art

BJJ stands for Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu. "Jiu-Jitsu" means "the gentle art," which sounds strange for something that involves grabbing people and putting them in holds. But it is called gentle because the whole idea of BJJ is to control someone without punching or kicking them. You use leverage, angles, and technique to put someone in a position where they cannot hurt you and they cannot escape — and then you let them go when they tap.

**Tapping is the most important rule in BJJ.** When your partner catches you in a hold — a choke, a joint lock, any position where you are stuck — you tap their body with your hand, or tap the mat, or say "tap." Your partner immediately lets go. You start again. No ego. No anger. No "that didn't count." Tapping is how you train safely. It is how you practice submissions a thousand times without anyone getting hurt. A student who refuses to tap is dangerous to themselves. A student who does not let go when their partner taps is removed from the mat.

**The basic positions:**

- **Guard:** You are on your back, and your partner is between your legs. In most situations, being on your back is bad. In BJJ, guard is actually a strong position because you can control your partner with your legs, sweep them (reverse the position), or catch them in a submission. You will start by learning to hold someone in your guard — wrapping your legs around their waist, controlling their posture with your hands, keeping them from getting past your legs.

- **Mount:** You are sitting on your partner's chest or stomach, straddling them with your knees on the mat. This is one of the most dominant positions in BJJ. The person on top has gravity, balance, and access to strikes (in a real fight) or submissions (in training). When you are in mount, your job is to stay there. When you are under mount, your job is to escape.

- **Side control:** You are on top of your partner, chest to chest, but to their side rather than straddling them. This is another dominant position. Your weight is on them, and their arms and legs are not in a good position to push you off.

At this age, you are learning to recognize these positions, get comfortable being in them (on top and on bottom), and begin to understand that some positions are better than others. You are not yet learning complex submissions or escapes — you are building the body awareness and comfort with contact that those skills will sit on top of later.

### Part 4: Boxing Basics — Hands Up, Chin Down

Boxing is the art of hitting with your fists and not getting hit back. At this age, you are not going to spar. You are not going to hit anyone. You are going to learn the basic mechanics of punching on pads and bags, and you are going to learn the most important defensive skill in boxing: keeping your hands up.

**Stance:** Similar to your wrestling stance, but with your hands higher. Your lead hand (left if you are right-handed, right if you are left-handed) is up near your chin. Your rear hand is tight against your cheek. Your elbows are tucked against your body. Your chin is slightly tucked — pointing down, not sticking out. A chin that sticks out is a target.

**The jab:** Your lead hand shoots straight out, fast, and comes straight back. It is not a power punch. It is a range-finder — it tells you how far away your target is, it keeps your opponent busy, and it sets up everything else. When you throw a jab, your hand goes out and comes back on the same line, and your other hand stays glued to your face. Practice this on a pad held by your mentor or training partner.

**The cross:** Your rear hand — the power hand — travels straight from your cheek to the target. You rotate your hips and shoulders as you throw it, which is where the power comes from. The power in a punch does not come from your arm. It comes from your legs, hips, and core rotating together. Your arm is just the delivery system.

**Padwork:** Your mentor or partner holds focus mitts. You throw jabs and crosses at the mitts. They call the combinations: "jab." "Jab-cross." "Jab-jab-cross." You focus on form — straight punches, hands back to your face immediately, good stance, balanced after every combination. Speed and power come later. Technique comes now.

### Part 5: De-escalation — The Most Important Martial Skill

Here is something that might surprise you: the most important thing you will learn in this entire packet is not a takedown, a submission, or a punch. It is how to avoid a fight in the first place.

**Walking away is not weakness.** If someone is trying to start a fight with you and you walk away, you have not lost. You have made the smartest decision available. A fight — a real fight, not training — is dangerous. People get hurt. People get in trouble. And almost every fight that happens between children or teenagers could have been avoided if someone had the self-control to walk away.

When you train martial arts, you develop a quiet confidence. You know you can handle yourself. And because you know that, you do not need to prove it. The student who brags about their training and looks for fights has missed the entire point. The student who trains hard, stays calm when someone is rude to them, and walks away from a confrontation they did not start — that student has understood what martial arts are actually for.

**When you cannot walk away:** Sometimes you cannot leave. Someone grabs you. Someone will not let you go. Someone is hurting someone else who cannot defend themselves. In those situations, your training matters. But notice that every one of those situations is a last resort — you have already tried to leave or de-escalate, and it did not work. Your training is a backup plan, not a first option.

**The order is always:**
1. Avoid — do not be where trouble is
2. De-escalate — use your words, stay calm, do not escalate
3. Walk away — leave if you can
4. Defend — use your training only when there is no other choice

### Part 6: What Training Teaches You About Yourself

Every time you step on the mat, you learn something about who you are. Not just about your body — about your character.

When you get pinned and feel frustrated, what do you do? Do you get angry and shove your partner? Or do you take a breath, think about what happened, and try again? When you are rolling with someone smaller or newer, do you smash them to feel powerful? Or do you roll lighter and let them work, because you remember what it was like to be new?

When you are tired and do not want to drill anymore, do you cut corners and do sloppy reps? Or do you do every rep like it matters, because you know that how you practice is how you will perform?

These are not just martial arts questions. They are life questions. The mat is a mirror. It shows you exactly who you are — not who you think you are, not who you want to be, but who you actually are when things are hard. And then it gives you the chance to become better.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Comfort with Physical Contact
**Developing well:** The student grapples willingly with partners. They are comfortable being held, pinned, and grabbed. They do not freeze or panic when a partner gets a dominant position. They engage with energy and curiosity.

**Developing slowly:** The student tenses up when grabbed. They refuse to engage or become very passive during grappling. They seem frightened of physical contact rather than simply unfamiliar with it.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student had sufficient exposure to rough-and-tumble play before formal training? Some children need more gradual introduction — wrestling games with a parent before wrestling with a peer. Is there an underlying issue (sensory sensitivity, past trauma) that requires a different approach? Patience and gradual exposure resolve most contact hesitancy.

### 2. Mat Etiquette
**Developing well:** The student bows on, shakes hands, listens to instruction, and treats partners with respect consistently, without reminders. They arrive clean and prepared.

**Developing slowly:** The student forgets rules, needs repeated reminders, talks during instruction, or treats the mat like a playground rather than a training space.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you enforcing the rules consistently? If mat etiquette is sometimes required and sometimes not, the student learns that the rules are optional. Consistency from the mentor produces consistency from the student.

### 3. Basic Position Recognition
**Developing well:** The student can name guard, mount, and side control when they see them. They can explain which position is better and why. They are beginning to recognize positions during live rolling, not just in demonstration.

**Developing slowly:** The student cannot identify positions or does not understand why one position is better than another. They grapple without positional awareness — just grabbing and pulling randomly.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you quizzing position recognition regularly? During rolling or video viewing, pause and ask: "What position is that? Who is winning? Why?" Repetition builds recognition.

### 4. De-escalation Understanding
**Developing well:** The student can articulate, in age-appropriate language, why walking away from a fight is smart and strong. They understand that their training is for defense, not for starting trouble. They can describe the avoid-de-escalate-leave-defend sequence.

**Developing slowly:** The student talks about using their skills to beat people up. They view martial arts training as making them tough or scary. They do not distinguish between self-defense and aggression.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you had the de-escalation conversation explicitly and repeatedly? Young children may need the message reinforced many times before it sticks, especially if peers or media are sending the opposite message. Role-play scenarios help: "Someone at school calls you a name. What do you do?" "Someone pushes you. What do you do?" "Someone won't stop pushing you and you can't leave. Now what?"
